title: Attention Mechanism
lesson:
If Transformers are the engine of every modern AI model, attention is the spark plug. It is the single idea that lets a model decide which earlier words matter when it reads each new word.

Attention answers one question, over and over: "Given everything I've seen so far, which parts should I focus on right now?"

The cocktail-party analogy

Imagine standing in a crowded, noisy room. Dozens of conversations happen at once, but you can tune your ears to the one person you care about and let the rest blur into background noise. Your brain is weighting the inputs: this voice gets 90% of your focus, that clatter of glasses gets almost none.

Attention does exactly this for words. When the model reads it in "The trophy didn't fit in the suitcase because it was too big", attention leans hard on trophy and ignores suitcase — figuring out what it refers to. That's the whole trick: relevant words get turned up, irrelevant ones get turned down.

Queries, keys, and values

Here's how the machinery works, in plain terms. Every word (really every token) is first turned into a list of numbers called an embedding — meaning stored as coordinates. From each embedding the model builds three smaller vectors:

  • Query — what this word is looking for.
  • Key — a label describing what a word is about.
  • Value — the actual content that gets mixed in.

Think of a library. The query is what you want ("books about pirates"). Each book wears a key on its spine — a label of its topic. You compare your query against every key to see how well they match. The better the match, the more attention weight that book gets. Then you read the values — the contents — blended together in proportion to how relevant each one was.

So for each word, attention does three steps:

  1. Compare its query to every key to get a score.
  2. Soften those scores into percentages that add up to 100% (a step called softmax, which just turns raw scores into a clean probability mix).
  3. Build a fresh summary by mixing the values using those percentages.

The result is a new version of that word that now "knows about" the words around it.

Self-attention, heads, and the future

When a sentence attends to itself — every word looking at every other word in the same sentence — we call it self-attention. Its superpower is comparing all words to all words in one parallel sweep, instead of crawling left-to-right like older models. That parallelism is a big reason these models train fast on GPUs.

Real models don't run attention once. They run several copies side by side, called attention heads ("multi-head attention"). Picture a panel of expert readers skimming the same paragraph: one head tracks grammar, another tracks who-did-what-to-whom, another tracks tone. Each head learns a different kind of relationship, and their findings get stitched back together.

⚠️ During text generation, a model can't peek at the future. Causal (masked) attention blindfolds each word so it can only attend to words before it — like rereading what you've written but not seeing the next page.

A tiny worked example

import numpy as np

# 3 words, each a 4-number embedding (toy values)
q = np.array([1.0, 0, 1, 0])      # query for one word
keys = np.array([[1.0, 0, 1, 0],  # key for word A (great match)
                 [0, 1, 0, 1],    # key for word B (no match)
                 [1, 0, 0, 0]])   # key for word C (partial)
scores = keys @ q                 # dot-product = similarity
weights = np.exp(scores) / np.exp(scores).sum()  # softmax
print(weights.round(2))           # e.g. [0.66 0.09 0.24]

Word A scored highest, so it gets ~66% of the focus; word B barely registers. Swap in the real value vectors and you'd blend them in those proportions to get the word's new meaning.

Why it matters in real products

Attention is why an LLM can answer questions about a document you pasted in: it links your question to the relevant lines. But it has a famous cost. Because every word looks at every other word, the work grows with the square of the length — double the text, roughly quadruple the attention compute.

That quadratic blow-up is the main reason a long context window is expensive, and it sparked tricks like Flash Attention (a faster, memory-frugal way to compute it) and the KV Cache (storing past keys and values so you don't recompute them every step).
mentalModel: Attention is a spotlight at a noisy party: for every word it reads, the model dims the irrelevant voices and shines bright light on the few earlier words that actually matter — then mixes only those into its understanding.
takeaways: Attention decides which earlier words each new word should focus on.
It matches a query to every key, then blends the values.
Cost grows with the square of length — long context is expensive.
mistakes: Thinking attention "remembers" things long-term. It doesn't store memories — it only re-weighs the words currently inside the context window. Once text scrolls out of the window, attention can't reach it at all.

Confusing attention with embeddings. An embedding is the fixed meaning of one word on its own; attention is the live process of blending words together based on context. Embeddings are the ingredients; attention is the mixing.

Assuming more attention heads always means smarter. Heads are specialists, but past a point extra heads add cost without adding skill, and many heads end up redundant. It's a design trade-off, not a dial you crank to 11.

Forgetting the quadratic cost. Doubling your input doesn't double attention work — it roughly quadruples it. People paste in huge prompts and are surprised by the latency and bill; long context is genuinely expensive.

Believing a text model can see future words. During generation, causal attention masks the future. Each word is predicted using only the words before it, never after — otherwise the model would cheat.

Treating attention weights as a reliable explanation. It's tempting to read the weights as "the model focused here because that's the reason for its answer." In practice attention maps are a rough hint, not a trustworthy account of why a model decided something.
exercise:
Copy the tiny NumPy snippet from the lesson and run it. Then experiment: change the q (query) vector and the three keys by hand and predict, before you run it, which word will get the most attention weight. Try to engineer a case where two words tie for focus (roughly 50/50) and a case where one word grabs nearly all of it. Write one sentence explaining what made the difference.

Stretch goal: Add a real values matrix (three 4-number vectors) and compute the final blended output as weights @ values. Then take a sentence like "The animal didn't cross the street because it was too tired" and, in plain English, describe which word it should attend to and why a model that picked street instead would answer the wrong question.
solution: The word whose key points in the most similar direction to your query wins, because attention scores are dot products — they're largest when two vectors line up. To force a tie, give two keys equal similarity to the query (e.g. make two keys identical). To make one dominate, give it a much larger overlap with the query than the others; softmax then exaggerates the gap into a near-100% share. For the stretch goal, weights @ values gives a weighted average of the value vectors, so the winning word's value dominates the result. In the sentence, it should attend to animal (animals get tired). A model attending to street would mis-resolve the reference and reason about a tired road — nonsense — which is exactly the kind of mistake good attention prevents.
summary: Attention lets a model decide, for every word it reads, which earlier words matter — turning relevant ones up and irrelevant ones down. It works by comparing a query against every key to get focus scores, then blending the matching values. Self-attention compares all words at once; multiple heads track different relationships; causal masking hides the future during generation. Its quadratic cost is why long context is expensive and why tricks like Flash Attention and the KV Cache exist.

diagram.caption: The query for "it" scores each key, and the values are blended in proportion — so "it" ends up mostly meaning "trophy".

quiz.q: In the library analogy, which part plays the role of the label on a book's spine?
quiz.explain: The key is a word's label, compared against the query to measure relevance. The value is the book's actual contents that get mixed in afterward.

quiz.q: You double the length of your input prompt. Roughly what happens to the attention compute?
quiz.explain: Every word attends to every other word, so cost grows with the square of the length. Doubling the text means about 4x the attention work — the reason long context is pricey.

quiz.q: Why does a text model use causal (masked) attention during generation?
options: To make training run on GPUs faster
So each word can only attend to earlier words, not future ones
To reduce the number of attention heads needed
To turn raw scores into percentages
quiz.explain: Causal masking blindfolds each word from the future so the model can't cheat by peeking at words it hasn't generated yet. The percentages step is softmax, a separate thing.

quiz.q: What is the main job of the softmax step in attention?
options: It builds the query, key, and value vectors
It masks out future words during generation
It turns raw similarity scores into focus percentages that sum to 100%
It stores past keys and values to avoid recomputation
quiz.explain: Softmax converts raw scores into a clean probability mix that adds up to 1, exaggerating the gaps so the strongest match dominates. Caching past keys/values is the KV Cache's job.

keyTerms.def: The mechanism that, for each word, decides how much focus to give every other word in the input, then blends them accordingly.
Attention where a sequence attends to itself — every word looks at every other word in the same text, all in one parallel pass.
Three vectors built from each word. The query is what you're seeking, the key is a word's label, and the value is its content that gets mixed in.
The percentage of focus one word gives another, after scores are passed through softmax so they add up to 100%.
A step that turns raw similarity scores into clean percentages that sum to 1, exaggerating big gaps so the strongest match stands out.
One independent copy of the attention computation. Models run many in parallel (multi-head attention), each specializing in a different kind of relationship.
A rule used in text generation that lets each word attend only to earlier words, never future ones, so the model can't cheat by seeing ahead.
Because every word attends to every other word, compute grows with the square of the input length — double the text means roughly four times the attention work.
